text: The surgeon begins a robotic low anterior resection for recto sigmoid cancer, isolating the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle near its takeoff from the aorta. The surgeon carefully creates a window proximal to the left colic artery, ensuring the vascular sheath is clear for sealing the vessel.

Hello, I'm sharing a robotic low anterior resection for a recto sigmoid cancer in a young male. This was found on a index colonoscopy for rectal bleeding, was at around a twenty five centimeters and above the peritoneal reflection. And he was worked up in a multidisciplinary fashion and elected to undergo a sigmoid resection.

The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection to isolate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle. The IMA is exposed just off its takeoff from the aorta, with careful identification of the ureter. The surgeon creates a window proximal to the left colic artery, visible on the cephalad aspect of the vessel, ensuring minimal tissue impedes the vascular seal.

The surgeon creates a window behind the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) using a sealing device, relieving tension on the vessel. Medial dissection is performed along the root of the mesentery towards the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), preparing for potential ligation. The surgeon then shifts to lateral dissection, lifting the descending colon mesentery off the kidney and progressing towards the splenic flexure, using the sealing device for hemostasis and manipulation.

The surgeon creates a window behind the vessel using a sealing device, relieving tension before proceeding with medial dissection along the root of the mesentery towards the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The surgeon then shifts to lateral dissection, lifting the descending colon mesentery off the kidney and advancing towards the splenic flexure. The sealing device is used for hemostasis and manipulation of the bowel and mesentery, ensuring no tension on the anastomosis.

The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection using scissors for precision, targeting the areolar plane. The dissection proceeds anteriorly below the peritoneal reflection to achieve an adequate distal margin for the rectal sigmoid cancer.

The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection using scissors for precision. The dissection focuses on achieving an adequate distal margin by going below the peritoneal reflection and proceeding anteriorly, staying within the visible areolar plane.

In this case, I believe there were fifty-six lymph nodes, eighteen of which were positive. The patient is getting set up for adjuvant therapy at this time. I found the planes in him to be a little bit more edematous and fibrotic. I actually wondered if this had to do with his tumor, which the endoscopic biopsies had a dense inflammatory reaction, desmoplastic reaction of the tumor itself, because this almost looked to me as if somebody had gotten neoadjuvant radiation with the amount of fibrosis in here.

The surgeon navigates through edematous and fibrotic tissue planes, likely due to the tumor's inflammatory reaction. They proceed down the left-sided plane and join anteriorly, cleaning off the rectal mesentery at a level more than six centimeters distal to the tumor, where a tattoo was placed.

The surgeon dissects down the left-sided plane and joins anteriorly, cleaning off the rectal mesentery at a level more than six centimeters distal to the tumor, marked by a tattoo for tumor-specific mesorectal excision.

The surgeon decides against transrectal extraction due to the large size of the cancer and bulky mesentery. After completing the transection, the specimen is moved aside, and the surgeon prepares to perform an intracorporeal sutured anastomosis.

The surgeon prepares for an intercorporeal anastomosis after completing the transection, moving the specimen aside. The proximal mesentery is taken up to the high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), aligning with the chosen proximal margin. Visible tattoos mark the proximal and distal boundaries relative to the tumor.

The surgeon takes the proximal mesentery near the high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), aligning it with the chosen proximal margin. Visible tattoos mark the proximal and distal locations relative to the tumor. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to assess the margins, with the stapler positioned for firing to secure the proximal margin, focusing on ensuring adequate blood supply.

The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) to assess the margins after taking the mesentery. A stapler is placed distally, requiring two firings to fit into the pelvis. The specimen is moved aside, and the surgeon checks the length in steep Trendelenburg position before proceeding with the anastomosis.

The surgeon places a 60mm stapler distally in the pelvis, requiring two firings to secure the staple line. The specimen is moved aside, and the surgeon checks the bowel length in steep Trendelenburg position. The proximal bowel is rotated clockwise to align the staple lines vertically, facilitating the creation of collatomies on the anti-mesenteric side of the sigmoid for suturing the posterior rows.

The surgeon rotates the proximal bowel clockwise to align the staple lines vertically, facilitating visibility of the anti-mesenteric side of the sigmoid for colotomy creation. The posterior rows of the anastomosis are joined, with the left hand assisting in counterclockwise rotation for better visualization. The surgeon clears pericolic fat and performs a proctotomy, starting the inner row of suturing in the posterior midline towards the patient's left. ICG is used for documentation, confirming the viability of the rectal and colon wall bridges between the staple line and cut edge.

The surgeon uses their left hand to rotate the bowel counterclockwise, aligning the staple lines for the anastomosis. The back layer of the bowel is joined at the staple lines. The surgeon clears pericolic fat to expose the bowel surface, then performs a colotomy followed by a proctotomy.

The surgeon begins suturing the inner row in the posterior midline, moving towards the patient's left. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to document the vascularization of the rectal and colon wall bridges between the staple line and the cut edge of the bowel, ensuring there is no visible ischemia.

The surgeon begins the second inner suture line, suturing towards the patient's right, ensuring full thickness bites of the tissue. The technique involves switching to a canal approach across the anterior aspect, aiming to prevent outward pouching of the mucosa.

The surgeon begins the second inner suture line, suturing towards the patient's right, ensuring full thickness bites of the tissue. The technique transitions to a canal across the anterior aspect, aiming to prevent outward pouching of the mucosa.

The surgeon encounters difficulty with a knot during the suturing process and decides to terminate the suture, bringing the original suture around to the left to connect with the cut suture. Additional throws are made to achieve hemostasis and prevent bleeding near the end of the anastomosis, ensuring no expanding hematoma forms in the mesentery.

The surgeon encounters a knot in the suture line and decides to terminate the suture, bringing the original suture to the left to connect with the cut suture. Additional throws are made to ensure hemostasis and prevent a hematoma in the mesentery. The surgeon then ties the sutures together and canals the original suture over the top to imbricate the inner suture line.

The surgeon ties the two suture lines together, then canals the original suture over the top to imbricate the inner suture line. A leak test is performed using a 19mm rigid scope, confirming the integrity of the anastomosis. The surgical field is cleaned, ensuring no tension remains.
The surgeon performs a leak test using a 19mm rigid scope to assess the integrity of the anastomosis. The surgical field is cleaned, and the tension of the sutures is checked visually.
